content.problemStatement:
A permutation PPP - [P1,P2,...,PN][P_1, P_2, ..., P_N][P1​,P2​,...,PN​] of size NNN is an array of size NNN consisting of integers 111, 222, .... NNN each exactly once, in some order.

Define f(P)=f(P) = f(P)= number of fixed points, i.e. number of iii such that (Pi=i)(P_i = i)(Pi​=i).

A cyclic shift of [P1,P2,....,PN][P_1, P_2, ...., P_N][P1​,P2​,....,PN​] is [PX+1,…,PN,P1,P2,...,PX][P_{X + 1}, \ldots, P_N, P_1, P_2, ..., P_{X}][PX+1​,…,PN​,P1​,P2​,...,PX​] for each 1≤X≤N1 \le X \le N1≤X≤N. Note that there are exactly NNN cyclic shifts.

Define g(P)=max⁡(f(Q))g(P) = \max(f(Q))g(P)=max(f(Q)) over all QQQ which are cyclic shifts of PPP.

Construct a permutation of size NNN which has minimum value of g(P)g(P)g(P) among all permutations of size NNN.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single integer, NNN, the size of the permutation.


Output Format
For each test case, output the permutation ppp which minimized the value g(p)g(p)g(p).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 500).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a permutation of size N that minimizes g(P). Print the N integers separated by a single space on one line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 the only permutation is [1] (g=1). For N=3 we swap the first two numbers and keep 3 fixed → [2,1,3]; its cyclic shifts have at most one fixed point, so g=1 which is optimal. For N=6 (even) we swap every adjacent pair → [2,1,4,3,6,5]; any cyclic shift contains at most two fixed points, achieving the optimal value g=2.

solution.approach:
We need a permutation P that makes the maximum number of fixed points among all its N cyclic shifts as small as possible. Observations:
- For N = 1 the only permutation has g(P) = 1.
- For even N we can pairwise swap adjacent elements: P = [2,1,4,3,6,5,…]. In any cyclic shift at most two positions can coincide, therefore the optimal value is g(P)=2.
- For odd N (N>1) we swap all pairs except the last element and keep the last element fixed: P = [2,1,4,3,6,5,…,N]. The fixed element guarantees at least one fixed point, but any shift contains at most one fixed point, so the optimal value is g(P)=1.
The construction is O(N) per test case and provably optimal because:
* For even N a permutation without any fixed point exists, but any cyclic shift of length N will align the two elements of at least one swapped pair, giving at least two fixed points. Hence g(P)≥2.
* For odd N any permutation must contain at least one fixed point (pigeon‑hole principle on parity of cycles), and the above construction achieves g(P)=1, which is optimal.
Algorithm:
1. Read T.
2. For each test case read N.
3. Initialise an array ans[1..N].
4. For i = 1; i+1 < N; i += 2 set ans[i] = i+1 and ans[i+1] = i.
5. If N is odd set ans[N] = N (the last element stays in place).
6. Output ans.
Complexities: Time O(T·N), Space O(N) per test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        ans = [0] * (N + 1)
        i = 1
        while i + 1 < N:
            ans[i] = i + 1
            ans[i + 1] = i
            i += 2
        if N % 2 == 1:
            ans[N] = N
        out_lines.append(' '.join(str(ans[i]) for i in range(1, N + 1)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
